2N board 259: Lead the ♣J. With ♣J-10-9 you hold three touching cards atop your one long suit, so the jack is free — it cannot cost, and it promises partner the ten and nine sitting behind it. Diamonds (♦J-7-6-4) is ragged by comparison; against a brisk 1♠–4♠ you attack from your solid suit and keep coming back to clubs every time you are on lead.
2N board 482: Lead the ♣K — the top of your ♣K-Q. From touching honours you lead the top card: it cannot cost a trick, it tells partner you hold the queen, and it goes straight to work on your five-card suit before declarer can set up discards.
2N_and_Transfers board 463: Lead the ♠Q — top of your ♠Q-J-10. A solid sequence is the safe, attacking lead against a suit contract: you knock out a high card and build winners in your long spade suit while the rest of the sequence stays poised over declarer.
2N_and_3C_Response board 382: Lead the ♣J — top of your ♣J-10-9. From a sequence you lead the top honour: it cannot cost, it promises the ten and nine, and it develops your six-card club suit before declarer can pitch losers on a side suit.
3N_Rebid_by_Opener board 189: Lead the ♥K — top of your ♥K-Q. Touching honours make the clean attacking lead: drive out declarer's ace and your queen, with the heart length behind it, takes over. A safe, constructive start.
2N_and_1_Minor board 258: Lead the ♠K — top of your ♠K-Q. From touching honours lead the top: knock out the ace, set up your five-card spade suit, and keep the queen working behind it.
1N board 27: Lead the ♠J — top of your ♠J-10-9 sequence. A three-card sequence is the textbook attacking lead: it cannot cost and it promises the run beneath. Your long diamonds tempt, but A-Q-10 is not a sequence — leading from it could cost a trick, so the spade sequence is the safer, more constructive start.
2N_and_Transfers board 12: Lead the ♠J — top of your ♠J-10. A two-card touching honour at the head of a four-card suit is a fine attacking lead: the jack cannot cost and promises the ten. With a void in their trump suit you have no ruffing value, so push to establish side-suit winners quickly.
2N_and_Balanced board 302: Lead the ♠10 — the top of your interior sequence ♠K-10-9. With a high honour, a gap, then a touching run, you lead the top of the run: it develops your long spades while the king stays poised over declarer, ready to score.
2N_and_Transfers board 201: Lead the ♣10 — top of the interior run in ♣Q-10-9. A higher honour, a gap, then a touching pair: lead the top of the run to build your clubs without burying the queen, which sits over declarer for later.
2N board 377: Lead the ♦J — top of your interior sequence ♦A-J-10. Lead the jack, the top of the touching run, not the ace: it develops your five-card diamond suit while the ace stays behind, ready to take a trick on its own.
After_1M_2M board 13: Lead the ♦K — top of your A-K. It is the safe, informative start: you keep the lead, and once dummy appears you will know whether to cash the ace, switch, or wait. With ♦A-K-10-5 you own the suit — lead the king, take stock, then decide.
2N board 72: Lead the ♥K from your A-K. Cashing the king keeps you on lead and lets you read dummy before committing, and your ♥A behind it means the suit cannot run away. See what comes down, then choose your second card.
2N board 1: Lead the ♦K — top of A-K. You keep the lead and get a look at dummy before deciding, and with the ♦A still in hand the suit is yours to control. Cash the king, then read the table.
After_1M_2M board 6: Lead the ♦K from A-K. Against their quiet 2♠ the king is the safe, talkative start — you hold the lead and see dummy, and the ♦A guards the suit. Take a look, then pick your next move.
1N board 270: Lead the ♣K from A-K. Against 2♥ the king is the safe, informative lead — you keep the lead, see dummy, and your ♣A still guards the suit. Cash it, then decide whether to continue clubs or shift.
Basic_Major board 35: Lead your singleton ♠3. Three small trumps and the ♣A for an entry is exactly the shape that wants a ruff: lead your lone spade now, and the moment partner can put you back in spades you trump one — a trick your hand makes no other way.
Basic_Major board 271: Lead your singleton ♥8. With three small trumps and the ♣A as an outside entry, go hunting for a ruff: lead your lone heart, and once you are back on lead a heart ruff is a trick your high cards alone cannot make.
After_1M_2M board 409: Lead your singleton ♦8. Short trumps and the ♥A for a sure entry make this a ruff hunt: lead your lone diamond, and if partner can win and return one — or read your shortness — you trump it for a trick your hand can't otherwise get.
After_1M_2M board 424: Lead your singleton ♥3. Short trumps and the ♣A for an entry make this a ruff hunt: lead your lone heart, and when you get in, a heart ruff is a trick your hand can't make any other way.
3N_Rebid_by_Opener board 467: Two good leads here, so either is accepted. The ♠J tops your ♠J-10 sequence — safe and attacking. Or cash the ♦K from your ♦A-K to keep the lead and look at dummy first. The spade builds a suit; the diamond keeps control and gathers information — pick the plan you like.
Basic_Overcall board 41: Two reasonable plans, so either is accepted. Cash the ♥K from your ♥A-K — safe, and you see dummy before committing. Or lead your singleton ♣7 hoping for a ruff: with three small trumps and the ♥A-K as entries, a club ruff could be the setting trick. The king is the solid choice, the singleton the ambitious one.
3N_Rebid_by_Opener board 75: Nothing leaps out here. Your honours are scattered — leading from ♠Q-10 or away from the ♣A would risk a trick — so attack your longest suit instead: the ♦3, fourth-best from ♦J-6-4-3-2. A workmanlike, low-risk start when no lead is obviously best.
2N_and_Balanced board 293: A tricky one — your only sequence (♠Q-J-10) is in their trump suit, no use on lead. With three ragged suits outside, lead the ♥9: top of nothing, telling partner you have no honour in hearts. A safe, honest card when there is nothing to attack.
2N_and_1_Minor board 139: No standout. Your singleton ♠10 is a trump, so no ruff there, and your red holdings are middling — so lead the ♣2, fourth-best from ♣J-8-4-2, a quiet stab at your longest side suit. The workmanlike choice with nothing clear-cut.
2N board 399: Awkward — you hold five trumps to the ace (♠A-9-7-6-5), so a singleton lead brings no ruff. Lead the ♥9, your singleton, as a passive, safe card and let your trump length fetch tricks on its own. Sit back and let the spades do the work.
3N_Rebid_by_Opener board 20: No clear lead. Leading from the ♦A (an unsupported ace) or away from the ♣J both court trouble, so choose safety: the ♠7, top of a doubleton. A passive card that gives nothing away while you wait to read declarer's plan.
2N board 344: Nothing jumps out. Your longest suit is clubs, so lead the ♣3 — fourth-best from ♣Q-7-5-3-2 — the constructive try at your own length rather than leading away from the ♠J into declarer. The workmanlike pick on a flat hand.
2N_and_1_Minor board 398: A close call. Your six-card heart suit is all spots and ♦Q-J-4 is not quite a lead, so the ♣Q, top of a doubleton, is the flexible stab: if partner has club help it works, and if not you have given little away.
2N_and_1_Minor board 158: No obvious lead, but you have a six-card suit: lead the ♥6, fourth-best from ♥Q-9-7-6-4-3. With three scattered queens and a singleton trump, attacking your own long suit is the soundest plan — build length and hope partner has a card there.
